item.title: Quebec issues invitations for permanent selection through PSTQ
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Québec, immigration, Skilled Worker Selection Program, provincial candidates, intermediate skills

Summary: On July 3, Québec’s Ministry of Immigration, Francisation and Integration (MIFI) conducted immigration selections for all four streams of the Skilled Worker Selection Program (PSTQ). A total of 501 provincial immigration candidates received invitations in this draw. The largest number of invitations were issued to candidates from stream 2, which focuses on workers with intermediate and manual skills. This selection process underscores Québec's ongoing commitment to attracting skilled labor to support its economy and communities. The MIFI aims to strengthen the province's workforce by targeting diverse skills and backgrounds to enhance productivity and innovation. These immigration selections reflect the government’s strategy to meet labor market needs and respond to demographic changes in the region. By inviting candidates from various skill levels, the province is working towards creating a more balanced and dynamic labor market. Overall, this initiative marks a significant step towards fulfilling Québec’s immigration goals and addressing the challenges posed by an aging population.

On July 3, Québec’s Ministry of Immigration, Francisation and Integration (MIFI) held immigration selections under all four streams of the Skilled Worker Selection Program (PSTQ). A total of 501 provincial immigration candidates received invitations in this draw, with the greatest share going to workers with intermediate and manual skills (stream 2). Learn more about your […]

item.title: Newfoundland and Labrador selects candidates for provincial immigration
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Newfoundland and Labrador, invitations, immigration, Provincial Nominee Program, Atlantic Immigration Program

Summary: On July 10, Newfoundland and Labrador conducted a draw issuing 57 invitations to immigration candidates through the Newfoundland and Labrador Provincial Nominee Program (NLPNP) and the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP). This event marks the eighth draw of the year and represents the province's smallest invitation round in 2026. The draw reflects the province's ongoing efforts to attract skilled workers and support its labor market needs. Candidates interested in enhanced Provincial Nominee Program (PNP) streams should assess their eligibility based on the current requirements and criteria set by the province. The low number of invitations may indicate a more selective process. Potential applicants are encouraged to stay informed about future draws and changes in immigration policies while preparing their applications to enhance their chances of receiving an invitation in subsequent rounds. Overall, the draw highlights the importance of provincial programs in addressing local labor shortages and promoting economic growth through immigration.

item.title: 10 questions to ask before hiring an immigration lawyer for Canadian citizenship by descent
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canadian citizenship, immigration lawyer, application, consultation, eligibility

Summary: If you believe you are eligible for Canadian citizenship by descent, hiring an immigration lawyer can significantly aid in your application process. It is advisable to ask essential questions before selecting a legal representative. Engaging with an immigration lawyer from firms like Cohen Immigration Law can provide clarity and ensure that you understand the nuances of the citizenship application. Key inquiries might include their experience with citizenship cases, their fee structure, the expected timeline for your application, and their approach to handling challenges. Other relevant questions involve understanding how they will communicate updates to you and what documents you will need to provide. Securing a free consultation can be beneficial to assess their expertise and gauge whether they are a good fit for your needs. Additionally, ensure the lawyer is reputable and recognized in the field of immigration law to increase the chances of a successful application outcome. Taking the time to ask these questions can enhance your understanding of the process and help you make an informed decision about hiring a representative for your citizenship application.

If you think you may be eligible for Canadian citizenship by descent, hiring an immigration lawyer can help with your application. These draws prioritize healthcare professionals, including nurses, offering them an efficient means of obtaining permanent residency. Another method for nurses to immigrate is through Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs), where provinces nominate skilled workers to meet local labor market demands, allowing for tailored pathways based on specific provincial needs. Furthermore, employer-sponsored work permits present another option, where employers in Canada can hire foreign nurses to fill vacancies and subsequently support their application for permanent residency. This multifaceted approach addresses the pressing demand for qualified healthcare professionals in Canada, enabling nurses to find opportunities suited to their skills and preferences while contributing to the healthcare system's efficacy. Overall, the variety of options available for qualified nurses enhances their chances of a successful migration experience while aiding Canada's healthcare workforce. With the ongoing need for healthcare workers, these pathways provide a structured and supportive approach for international nurses seeking to build their careers in Canada.

item.title: Your ancestor’s Canadian birth could make you a citizen, thanks to Bill C-3
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: birthright citizenship, U.S. Supreme Court, Donald Trump, countries, concept

Summary: Birthright citizenship has gained significant attention following the U.S. Supreme Court's recent decision to overturn Donald Trump's efforts to eliminate it. This judicial ruling highlights the contentious nature of the policy in the United States, where children born on American soil are granted automatic citizenship, regardless of their parents' immigration status. However, the principle of birthright citizenship is not exclusive to the U.S.; many countries worldwide also recognize varying forms of this legal entitlement. The debate surrounding birthright citizenship often reflects broader discussions about immigration policy, national identity, and human rights. Critics argue that it incentivizes illegal immigration, while supporters view it as a fundamental right that upholds the principle of equality. As the legal landscape evolves and political tensions rise, the future of birthright citizenship remains uncertain in the United States and beyond. The ruling reinforces the notion that citizenship laws can significantly impact the lives of individuals and shape the national discourse surrounding immigration issues. Understanding how other nations approach birthright citizenship can provide valuable insights into potential reforms and the ongoing debate in the U.S.

Birthright citizenship has been in the spotlight this past week after the U.S. Supreme Court struck down Donald Trump’s order seeking to end it in the United States. While the president and the court may not agree on the issue, birthright citizenship isn’t a concept unique to the U.S. Dozens of countries recognize some form […]

The post Your ancestor’s Canadian birth could make you a citizen, thanks to Bill C-3 first appeared on CIC News.

Summary: Canada has announced an expansion of its Express Entry immigration program, introducing new priority categories aimed at addressing labor shortages in key sectors. These priority categories include healthcare, STEM (science, technology, engineering, and mathematics), transportation, trades, research, and other skilled professions. This strategic move is designed to attract skilled workers in areas where there is significant demand, enhancing the country’s ability to meet its economic and social needs. By tailoring immigration pathways to specific sectors, Canada's government aims to streamline the entry process for qualified individuals, thus expediting their integration into the Canadian workforce. The initiative is particularly timely, considering ongoing challenges related to labor shortages in essential services and the growing demand for professionals in technological and scientific fields. This expansion of Express Entry not only boosts opportunities for potential immigrants but also reinforces Canada’s commitment to fostering a diverse and skilled labor market that supports economic growth and innovation. Additionally, this approach aligns with the government's broader goals of promoting regional development and ensuring a balanced distribution of skilled labor across the provinces.

item.title: Canada Immigration Service - YouTube
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: PNP, Canada, PR, immigration, Express Entry

Summary: The Provincial Nominee Program (PNP) is recognized as one of Canada's fastest pathways to permanent residency (PR). It is designed for individuals who wish to settle in specific provinces and territories, allowing them to gain nominations from provincial governments. These nominations enhance a candidate's Express Entry profile, which is a system used to manage applications for PR. Each province has different streams under the PNP, targeting varying skills, occupations, and labor market needs. Candidates can apply through the Express Entry system after receiving a nomination, which significantly increases their Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS) score. Additionally, the PNP promotes regional economic growth by addressing labor shortages. It is essential for applicants to meet both the province's criteria and the federal immigration requirements. Potential immigrants should research individual provinces to find suitable streams that match their skills and qualifications. The PNP is an attractive option for many due to its quicker processing times compared to other pathways. In summary, the PNP provides a structured route to PR for applicants, facilitating successful settlement in Canada's diverse provinces.

Each program has specific eligibility criteria, which candidates must meet to create a profile. Applicants are ranked based on a points system, and those with the highest scores are invited to apply for permanent residency. In addition to Express Entry, foreign workers can seek job opportunities in Canada that may require a Labor Market Impact Assessment (LMIA), a document that shows that there is a need for a foreign worker to fill a job position. The provinces of Alberta and British Columbia, among others, have unique provincial nominee programs that support specific immigration needs aligned with labor market demands. Life in Canada is diverse and vibrant, centered around multiculturalism, with various communities offering different lifestyles. The new framework will focus on 94 specific job roles that are identified as essential for the Canadian labor market. By easing work permit requirements, Canada aims to attract skilled workers who can contribute to various sectors facing labor shortages. The changes signify a proactive approach by the Canadian government to address economic needs while simultaneously welcoming a diverse range of talents into the country. The priority categories will likely target fields such as healthcare, technology, and trades, thereby enhancing the country's workforce stability and growth potential. With these reforms, Canada seeks to strengthen its immigration strategy, ensuring that it remains competitive in attracting global talent. Overall, the 2026 update to the Express Entry system marks a significant shift in how Canada manages immigration, with a clear focus on meeting labor demands through expedited processes.

item.title: The Supreme Court just narrowed the field for trans athletes in America. Some of those families are already Canadian citizens
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: US Supreme Court, transgender athletes, state bans, decision, West Virginia

Summary: On June 30, the US Supreme Court delivered a significant ruling by upholding state bans on transgender student athletes participating in girls' and women's sports. The decision, which was reached with a 6-3 majority in the cases of West Virginia v. B.P.J. and Little v. Hecox, affirmed the legality of laws enacted in West Virginia and Idaho. This ruling is particularly impactful as it not only maintains the existing restrictions in these two states but also strengthens similar legislative measures already in place across more than two dozen states. By endorsing these bans, the Supreme Court has indicated a broader trend towards limiting the rights of transgender athletes in the context of competitive sports. This decision also reflects ongoing national debates surrounding gender identity, equality in sports, and the rights of student athletes. The ramifications of this ruling are likely to be felt across schools and sports organizations as they navigate the implications for inclusion and fairness in athletics, setting a precedent that could influence future legal challenges and legislative actions in other states.

On June 30, the US Supreme Court upheld state bans on transgender student athletes playing girls’ and women’s sports. The 6-3 decision in West Virginia v. B.P.J. and Little v. Notably, it stands out as the only North American city to make it onto the EIU’s prestigious top 10 list, highlighting its exceptional quality of life compared to other global cities. Factors contributing to its livability likely include the city's stunning natural scenery, comprehensive healthcare system, vibrant cultural scene, and commitment to sustainability. The EIU's rankings are based on various criteria, emphasizing the importance of safety, education, infrastructure, and environmental factors in determining the overall livability of urban areas. Vancouver's consistent performance underscores its appeal to residents and visitors alike, cementing its status as a premier city in which to live. As cities around the world continue to evolve, Vancouver’s enduring presence in the top tier of livability rankings showcases its resilience and attractiveness as a major urban center.

Summary: The episode discusses significant upcoming reforms in Canada's Express Entry immigration system, highlighting the current scenario where job offers hold no value in the Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS). However, the episode hints at potential changes on the horizon, suggesting that future reforms may assign value to job offers, enhancing their importance in the immigration process. The discussion emphasizes the need for applicants to understand the evolving landscape of immigration policies and how they may impact their applications. item.title: Low-wage LMIA processing restrictions lifted for eight regions, including Halifax, Winnipeg, and Regina
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: LMIA, low-wage, regions, government, work permit

Summary: Beginning July 10, the federal government will initiate the processing of low-wage Labour Market Impact Assessment (LMIA) applications in eight specific regions across Canada, which include Halifax, Winnipeg, and Regina. This initiative aims to address labor shortages in these areas by allowing employers to hire foreign workers for low-wage positions. However, there are notable changes in eligibility for LMIA processing. Regions such as Saskatoon and Kamloops have been deemed ineligible for low-wage LMIA processing and will remain so until at least October 9. This restriction may impact employers in these areas looking to recruit certain low-wage workers. As a result, employers in the eligible regions are encouraged to take advantage of this opportunity. Those interested in navigating the application process or seeking advice on work permits can schedule a free consultation to discuss their options. The changes reflect the government's ongoing efforts to balance labor market needs while managing the immigration system effectively.

Beginning July 10, the federal government will start processing low-wage Labour Market Impact Assessment (LMIA) applications in eight regions across Canada, including in Halifax, Winnipeg, and Regina. item.title: My spouse found out they’re a Canadian citizen by descent. Am I eligible for citizenship, too?
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada, citizenship, Americans, ancestry, IRCC

Summary: After a significant change to Canada's citizenship law on December 15, 2025, millions of Americans have the opportunity to claim Canadian citizenship through their ancestral ties. This change opens the possibility for many individuals, particularly those with Canadian heritage, to obtain proof of citizenship. If an American's spouse is eligible due to their ancestry, they can initiate the application process for proof of their Canadian citizenship. This process is facilitated by Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC), which is responsible for confirming citizenship status. Once IRCC verifies their citizenship, the individual will receive official documentation confirming their Canadian citizenship. This changes the landscape for many Americans by potentially granting them the rights and privileges of Canadian citizens, including access to healthcare, education, and the right to live and work in Canada. The law aims to promote inclusivity and strengthen the connection between Canada and its citizens living abroad. Overall, this legislative shift presents a unique opportunity for eligible Americans to explore their Canadian identity and heritage.

After Canada’s citizenship law changed on December 15, 2025, millions of Americans are waking up to the reality that they are also Canadian citizens. If your spouse is one of them, through their ancestry, they can apply for proof of Canadian citizenship. item.title: Ottawa reports increased non-compliance amid TFWP crackdown
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: employers, compliance, Temporary Foreign Worker Program, non-compliant, fiscal year

Summary: A recent report indicates a growing trend of employers breaking rules related to Canada’s Temporary Foreign Worker Program (TFWP). From April 1, 2025, to March 31, 2026, a total of 1,488 compliance inspections were conducted, revealing that 12% of employers were found to be non-compliant. This marks an increase from the previous fiscal year, 2024-2025, where the non-compliance rate stood at 10%. The increase in non-compliance may raise concerns about the integrity and effectiveness of the TFWP, which is designed to address labor shortages while ensuring that foreign workers are treated fairly. Employers participating in the program are expected to adhere to specific regulations to protect workers’ rights and maintain program integrity. The rise in non-compliance suggests that more stringent oversight and enforcement may be needed to ensure that employers adhere to the rules. This report highlights the necessity for ongoing monitoring and potential reforms in the TFWP to better safeguard foreign workers and maintain fair labor practices in Canada.

A greater share of employers have been breaking the rules of Canada’s Temporary Foreign Worker Program (TFWP). Summary: Processing times for Labour Market Impact Assessments (LMIAs) have risen for both the high-wage and low-wage streams of the Temporary Foreign Worker Program (TFWP). This has led to longer wait periods for employers seeking to hire temporary foreign workers under these categories. In contrast, three specific streams have experienced a reduction in LMIA wait times. The notable exception is the permanent resident stream, which has seen a decrease of approximately two weeks in processing times. This change may benefit individuals seeking to transition from temporary to permanent residency in Canada. Employers and potential workers are encouraged to stay informed about these developments, especially when planning to apply for work permits. Additionally, it is recommended to consider scheduling a free consultation for further guidance on the application process, particularly through Employment and Social Development Canada (ESDC), the federal department responsible for overseeing these programs. Such consultations can provide valuable insights into navigating the complexities of the LMIA process and understanding the current trends in processing times.

The situation has prompted individuals seeking citizenship by descent to consider seeking professional assistance, especially with the complexities introduced by the review process. Those interested in applying for proof of Canadian citizenship are encouraged to seek a free consultation to navigate the evolving legal landscape effectively. Applicants are advised to remain vigilant and proactive in understanding both their rights and the regulatory environment surrounding citizenship by descent in Canada.

Such measures are essential for fostering a diverse and vibrant workforce that meets the demands of employers across the country. Overall, this draw represents a significant opportunity for qualified individuals with French language skills to establish themselves in Canada and contribute positively to the nation’s growth.

This high cutoff significantly limits the chances of individuals without a provincial nomination for immigration. Many PGWP holders lack the necessary provincial nomination or additional qualifications that would allow them to achieve the CRS scores required for successful immigration under the current system. There is growing concern that if the CRS cutoffs remain this high, many talented individuals will be discouraged from pursuing their immigration goals. This was followed by the Canadian Experience Class (CEC) draw on July 7, which focused on inviting candidates with work experience in Canada. The frequent draws indicate a robust approach by the IRCC in managing the Express Entry system, aiming to invite a diverse range of skilled immigrants. This pattern of draws suggests a strategic effort to meet labor market demands, enhance economic growth, and support community needs across Canada. The recent activities highlight the government's commitment to facilitating immigration pathways, thereby responding to the increasing demand for skilled labor in various sectors. Never before has there been such a concentrated series of draws within a short timeframe in recent history, showcasing the government's dynamic approach to immigration and its significance in contributing to the Canadian workforce.

item.title: Canada and the Northwest Territories join forces to support the growth of Francophone communities
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada, Francophone, immigration, communities, retention

Summary: Canada is focusing on Francophone immigration as a means to strengthen community ties and acknowledge the vital roles that Francophone and bilingual newcomers play in enhancing the Francophonie. In collaboration with the Government of the Northwest Territories, the Canadian government aims to promote demographic and economic development in these regions. Efforts are being made to retain qualified Francophone and bilingual individuals, recognizing their contributions to local economies and cultures. The initiative highlights the importance of ensuring that these newcomers can integrate successfully into their communities, thereby fostering a diverse and thriving environment. By addressing the specific needs of Francophone immigrants, both governments seek to attract talented individuals who can support the growth and sustainability of the Northwest Territories. Overall, this strategy underscores a commitment to linguistic diversity and the social and economic advantages that arise from a multicultural populace. The objective is not only to enhance the demographic landscape but also to create a rich tapestry of cultural interactions that can benefit all residents of the region.
Canada is making Francophone immigration a force that unites communities and recognizes the contribution of Francophone and bilingual newcomers to the growth of the Francophonie. With this in mind, to promote the demographic and economic development of communities in the Northwest Territories, the governments of Canada and the Northwest Territories are working together to ensure the retention of qualified Francophone and bilingual talent.

item.title: Canada holds first Canadian Experience Class draw of July
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Immigration, ITA, Express Entry, CRS, Canadian Experience Class

Summary: Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) has conducted a new draw through the Express Entry system, issuing a total of 2,000 Invitations to Apply (ITAs). This draw specifically targeted candidates in the Canadian Experience Class. To be eligible for this round, candidates were required to meet a minimum Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS) score of 517. In order to be considered for selection, individuals needed to have created an Express Entry profile, signifying their intent to immigrate to Canada. This draw reflects the ongoing efforts by the Canadian government to attract skilled workers and support the integration of individuals who have previous work experience in the country. The Express Entry system continues to serve as a vital channel for skilled immigration, allowing Canada to fill labor shortages and promote economic growth through a diverse workforce. The IRCC regularly updates its draws and criteria, making it essential for prospective immigrants to stay informed about their chances and prepare their applications accordingly.

The FMCSP is particularly beneficial for students as it provides a clearer path to settle in Canada. By focusing on French-speaking talent, the program seeks to enhance the vitality of Francophone communities across the country. This extension demonstrates the government's commitment to supporting diversity and the integration of French-speaking individuals, who contribute significantly to Canada's cultural landscape. Furthermore, the FMCSP aligns with broader immigration goals to attract skilled workers and fill labor shortages in various sectors. The announcement has generated positive feedback, highlighting its potential to enrich communities and stimulate economic growth through increased immigration of qualified individuals. In summary, the FMCSP represents a significant opportunity for French-speaking students, ensuring a more straightforward approach to achieving permanent residency in Canada.

item.title: Your Canadian citizenship ceremony: what to expect and how to prepare
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canadian citizen, naturalized, citizenship ceremony, virtual, Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada

Summary: The final step to becoming a naturalized Canadian citizen is attending a citizenship ceremony, which can take place in two formats: virtually or in person. Virtual ceremonies are the most common format and are conducted via video conference. In-person ceremonies occur in designated ceremony rooms at local Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) offices. Each format may have different requirements that applicants must fulfill prior to the ceremony. Attending this ceremony is essential for individuals who have completed the application process and passed the citizenship test, as it provides them with a formal opportunity to receive their citizenship certificate. This ceremony is also a celebration of their new status as Canadian citizens and typically includes an oath-taking component, where applicants pledge allegiance to Canada. Overall, the citizenship ceremony marks the culmination of a lengthy process and symbolizes the integration of newcomers into Canadian society.

The final step to becoming a naturalized Canadian citizen is attending a citizenship ceremony, which can involve different requirements depending on the format. Citizenship ceremonies are conducted virtually via a video conference (most common) or in person in a ceremony room at a local Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) office. item.title: Peter Fragiskatos, MP, highlights launch of National Food Security Strategy
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada, economy, food security, Peter Fragiskatos, agriculture

Summary: The Government of Canada is concentrating on enhancing the economy and improving affordability for its citizens. Peter Fragiskatos, who serves as the Parliamentary Secretary to the Minister of Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship, announced the unveiling of the National Food Security Strategy on behalf of Heath MacDonald, the Minister of Agriculture and Agri-Food. This initiative aims to address food insecurity across the nation, ensuring that all Canadians have reliable access to sufficient, safe, and nutritious food. The strategy encompasses various measures, including strengthening local food systems, supporting farmers, and promoting sustainable practices in agriculture. By focusing on these areas, the government intends to bolster the resilience of food supply chains and create a more stable economic environment for both producers and consumers. The National Food Security Strategy reflects the commitment of the Canadian government to tackle challenges in food accessibility and to foster a healthy population. This comprehensive approach is designed to not only alleviate immediate concerns but also to create long-term solutions that empower communities and enhance the overall quality of life for Canadians.
The Government of Canada is focused on building a stronger economy and making life more affordable for Canadians. Today, Peter Fragiskatos, Parliamentary Secretary to the Minister of Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship, on behalf of the Honourable Heath MacDonald, Minister of Agriculture and Agri-Food, highlighted the launch of the National Food Security Strategy.

item.title: Canada and Manitoba join forces to give new momentum to Francophone immigration
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada, Francophone, immigration, communities, economy

Summary: Canada is dedicated to enhancing Francophone immigration and aims to attract and retain top global talent. Recognizing the significant demographic and economic challenges faced by Francophone minority communities, the Canadian and Manitoba governments are collaborating to bolster these official language minority communities. Their strategy focuses on addressing the specific labor needs of these communities, thereby contributing to their economic growth. Additionally, the initiative aims to promote the linguistic vitality of Francophone populations, ensuring that French language and culture are preserved and celebrated. This partnership reflects a broader commitment to supporting the integration of Francophones into the workforce while also enhancing their overall quality of life. By fostering economic development and addressing the essential workforce demands, the initiative seeks to create a thriving environment for Francophone communities, ensuring their sustainability and contributing to the diversity of Canada’s multicultural landscape. Through these efforts, both levels of government are working to create opportunities that support the economic and linguistic well-being of Francophone communities, ultimately benefiting the entire Canadian society.
Canada is committed to supporting Francophone immigration and is focused on attracting and retaining the world’s best talent. As Francophone minority communities face significant demographic and economic challenges, the governments of Canada and Manitoba are joining forces to strengthen official language minority communities by addressing their labour needs and fostering their economic and linguistic vitality.

item.title: Seven reasons you think you don’t qualify for Canadian citizenship by descent (and why you probably do)
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canadian citizenship, Bill C-3, citizenship by descent, first-generation limit, free consultation.

Summary: Bill C-3 came into effect on December 15, 2025, and it has significant implications for Canadian citizenship, particularly for those born outside Canada. This legislation removed the first-generation limit on citizenship by descent, which previously restricted the ability to obtain Canadian citizenship to only one generation born outside the country. As a result of this change, individuals who may have previously thought they were ineligible for Canadian citizenship due to being born abroad can now potentially claim it. The modification allows for a broader scope of individuals to explore their eligibility for Canadian citizenship based on their familial connections. Furthermore, there are resources available for those interested in applying for proof of their Canadian citizenship, including free consultations to navigate the application process. This change represents an important opportunity for many individuals with Canadian ancestry, expanding their rights to citizenship and fostering a stronger connection to Canada. Overall, the removal of the first-generation limit is a significant step toward inclusivity in Canadian citizenship laws.

You might actually be a Canadian citizen and not even realize it. On December 15, 2025, Bill C-3 came into effect, removing the first-generation limit that previously cut off citizenship by descent after one generation born outside Canada*. Get a Free Consultation on Applying for Proof of Canadian Citizenship That change opened the door for […]

The post Seven reasons you think you don’t qualify for Canadian citizenship by descent (and why you probably do) first appeared on CIC News.

item.title: British Columbia holds provincial immigration draw targeting entrepreneurs across two PR pathways
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: British Columbia, Entrepreneur Immigration, PNP streams, business, selection round

Summary: On June 30, British Columbia held a new selection round for its Entrepreneur Immigration (EI) category. The province specifically targeted entrepreneurs interested in establishing new businesses or acquiring and expanding existing ones within British Columbia. This particular round marked the seventh selection event of 2026, focusing on attracting individuals who can contribute to the province's economy through their entrepreneurial ventures. The selection process assesses candidates' business experience, proposed business plans, and their ability to meet investment requirements. Entrepreneurs interested in enhancing their skills and understanding of the provincial nominee program (PNP) streams are encouraged to evaluate their eligibility. The EI category is a pathway for individuals to gain permanent residency in Canada, provided they meet the criteria set by the province. With a growing emphasis on fostering innovation and business growth, British Columbia continues to support entrepreneurs looking to make impactful contributions to the local economy. Summary: Canada has announced a decrease in immigration numbers for the years 2026-2028, adjusting its targets to reflect new policy directions. The new goal is to welcome 380,000 newcomers, which is a shift from previous years' higher targets. Despite the reduction in overall numbers, significant changes are coming to the Express Entry system, which governs immigration for skilled workers. The updates will introduce more flexibility in the selection criteria, allowing for the consideration of varied skills and experiences rather than strictly based on job offers. This could enhance opportunities for applicants with in-demand qualifications who may not necessarily have a job lined up in Canada. Additionally, provincial nominee programs (PNPs) may play a larger role in recruiting skilled professionals suited to local labor market needs. These adjustments aim to better match immigration policy with labor market demands, focusing on long-term integration and success of newcomers. Stakeholders and advocates are encouraged to engage with the evolving policies to ensure that both the economy and community needs are met effectively. Overall, the changes present a mixed approach to immigration that seeks to balance numbers and essential workforce needs.

item.title: A common reason Express Entry candidates miss their ITA deadline, and how to avoid it
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Express Entry, ITA, permanent residence, police certificates, documentation

Summary: When an Express Entry candidate receives an Invitation to Apply (ITA), they have a strict timeline of 60 days to submit a complete application for permanent residence (PR). This application must be thorough and include all required documentation. Among these documents, police certificates are particularly critical yet can often take an extended period to obtain. If candidates do not secure their police certificates in time, they risk jeopardizing their PR application. It is essential for candidates to start gathering these documents early and to understand the processing times involved. Failing to meet the 60-day deadline can result in the rejection of the application altogether. Consequently, candidates are advised to prioritize obtaining their police certificates and other necessary documents as soon as they receive their ITA. Being proactive in this regard can help ensure a smooth application process and minimize the risk of delays or refusal of their permanent residence application.

Once an Express Entry candidate receives an invitation to apply (ITA), they must submit a complete application for permanent residence (PR) within 60 days, with all required documentation included. item.title: Express Entry Draw #422 | 4,000 Healthcare and Social Service Professionals Invited Again!
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Express Entry, Canada, permanent residency, point-based system, immigration

Summary: Express Entry is a system implemented by the Canadian government to streamline the process of managing applications for Canadian permanent residency. It operates on a point-based system, where candidates are assessed based on various factors including age, education, work experience, and language proficiency. The highest-scoring candidates in the Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS) are invited to apply for permanent residency during regular draws conducted by Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC). The program is designed to attract skilled workers to Canada, filling labor shortages and contributing to the economy. Applicants must first create an online profile, after which they receive a score based on their credentials. Various factors can enhance a candidate's score, such as securing a job offer from a Canadian employer or obtaining a provincial nomination. Express Entry encompasses several immigration programs, including the Federal Skilled Worker Program, Federal Skilled Trades Program, and Canadian Experience Class. It offers a fast-track route to permanent residency, significantly reducing processing times compared to traditional application methods, thus making Canada an appealing destination for potential immigrants.
Express Entry is a system used by the Canadian government to manage applications for Canadian permanent residency. Express Entry uses a point-based ...

item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Express Entry, ITAs, Provincial Nominee Program, allocations, reform

Summary: Provinces across Canada are pressing the federal government, specifically Ottawa, to increase the allocations for the Provincial Nominee Program (PNP) and to reform the Express Entry system. The PNP allows provinces to select immigrants who meet their specific labor market needs, and it has become a crucial pathway for many who wish to settle in Canada. However, the current allocation limits are seen as restrictive, hindering provinces from fully utilizing this program to address local workforce shortages. Advocates argue that increasing the number of nominations would not only benefit the provinces but also the national economy by attracting skilled labor. Moreover, there is a call for a reform of the Express Entry system to make it more responsive to the changing demographics and economic demands of various regions across Canada. Stakeholders believe that both increasing allocations and reforming the system would enhance Canada's immigration strategy, making it more equitable and effective. Dialogue between provincial representatives and federal authorities is ongoing, reflecting the urgency of improving the PNP and Express Entry for future immigration planning.

item.title: How Canada’s citizenship by descent law helped one footballer make Canada’s FIFA World Cup squad
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Alfie Jones, Canadian citizen, national soccer team, citizenship laws, grandmother

Summary: Alfie Jones, an England-born football player, recently became a Canadian citizen, which enabled him to join Canada’s national soccer team. His eligibility for citizenship stems from his grandmother, who was born in Hillcrest, Alberta. Until last year, Canada’s laws limited citizenship by descent, making it impossible for individuals with only a grandparent born in Canada to obtain citizenship. This situation changed with a revision in the citizenship laws, allowing individuals with a Canadian grandparent to acquire citizenship. Jones’ situation highlights the significance of these changes, as they open doors for many individuals with Canadian heritage to represent Canada on international platforms. Grants of citizenship through descent have become increasingly relevant, reflecting Canada’s commitment to welcoming individuals connected to its history. Jones’s journey illustrates the intersection of sports and national identity, as he embarks on this new chapter in his career, proudly representing Canada in soccer. His story resonates with many who aspire to connect with their roots and contribute to Canada’s diverse sporting culture.

England-born football player Alfie Jones became a Canadian citizen last year, and earned a place on Canada’s national soccer team – all because of a grandmother born in Hillcrest, Alberta. For around 16 years, having a Canadian grandparent wasn’t enough to pass citizenship down the family line. But Canada’s changed its citizenship by descent laws […]

item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Ontario, PR, OINP, Employer Pathway, Immigration

Summary: The Ontario Immigrant Nominee Program (OINP) provides pathways for individuals to obtain Permanent Residency (PR) in Ontario without relying on Express Entry. One of the key pathways under the OINP is the Employer Pathway, which allows employers to nominate foreign workers for PR based on their skills and experience. To qualify, candidates must have a permanent job offer from an Ontario employer, along with relevant work experience in their field. It is crucial for applicants to ensure that their job offer meets the OINP requirements, such as being in a skilled occupation and having a salary that aligns with industry standards. Additionally, candidates should prepare for the application process by gathering necessary documentation, including proof of work experience and educational credentials. The OINP may also require candidates to demonstrate their intent to reside in Ontario. Overall, the OINP Employer Pathway offers a valuable alternative route to PR for individuals who may not be eligible for Express Entry, especially those already working in Ontario or with skills that are in demand within the province.

item.title: Millions of Americans have two independence days this year. Most don’t know it
item.content:encoded:

Summary: In the week marking July 1, Canada Day, and July 4, Independence Day, two flags are raised, symbolizing national pride. Recently, Canada has implemented new citizenship policies allowing American citizens to hold dual citizenship. This legal development means that millions of Americans may now own Canadian citizenship as well, without being fully aware of it. The announcement is significant, as it encourages dual nationality and strengthens ties between the two neighboring countries. It highlights the rich cultural exchange and connection that exists between Canada and the United States. The change in citizenship laws accommodates those who have historically had ties to Canada, such as family connections or long-standing residency. Many Americans who own both flags are encouraged to explore this newfound recognition of their Canadian identity, boosting the notion of dual citizenship. This legal fact not only opens doors for Americans in terms of residency and benefits but also fosters greater mutual understanding and appreciation between the citizens of both nations. As both holidays approach, the celebration of identity and belonging takes center stage, reminding individuals of the privileges and connections that come with citizenship.

Two flags go up this week, three days apart. Canada marks Canada Day on July 1, and the United States celebrates its independence on July 4. item.title: US to deny entry to foreign pregnant women after SC verdict on birthright citizenship?
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: US, foreign pregnant women, entry denial, birthright citizenship, Supreme Court

Summary: The United States has announced plans to deny entry to foreign pregnant women following a recent Supreme Court verdict concerning birthright citizenship. This policy change is aimed at preventing individuals from traveling to the U.S. to give birth and thus secure citizenship for their children. The ruling reflects ongoing debates around immigration and the rights conferred by birthright citizenship as outlined in the 14th Amendment. The administration argues that this move is necessary to uphold immigration laws and manage resource allocations effectively. Critics of the policy contend that it discriminates against pregnant women and undermines family unity. Additionally, there are concerns about the implications for international relations, as the policy may deter legitimate travelers. This significant shift in immigration policy may lead to increased scrutiny and enforcement at points of entry, impacting not only pregnant travelers but also those accompanying them. Legal experts are evaluating potential challenges to the policy, suggesting that it could face opposition in the courts. Overall, the decision adds another layer to the complex landscape of U.S. immigration policy and the contentious discourse surrounding citizenship rights.

Initially, the IRCC identified approximately 100 certificates that raised potential eligibility concerns. The review is currently ongoing for the remaining 67 cases, which may involve further scrutiny to determine their eligibility for citizenship. The department's proactive approach aims to ensure that individuals who rightfully qualify for citizenship are recognized and that any discrepancies are addressed appropriately. IRCC’s transparency in communicating these developments serves to reassure those affected, as well as the broader public, about the integrity of the citizenship issuance process under the provisions of Bill C-3. Summary: Canadian citizenship is determined by bloodlines, with specific rules that historically impacted descendants of individuals who lived in Canada before 1947. This year marks a significant change, as 1947 was when Canada first established citizenship as a formal legal status, outlining clear criteria for who would obtain it. Ancestors who lived in Canada prior to this establishment may have faced exclusion from citizenship. This historical context is crucial for understanding how citizenship has evolved and affected families over generations. Recent discussions and potential reforms aim to address these historical oversights, allowing those whose ancestors were denied citizenship due to these early rules to reclaim their status. The significance of 1947 continues to resonate in contemporary discussions regarding citizenship rules and lineage, underscoring the importance of recognizing historical injustices and finding ways to rectify them. As policymakers and advocates focus on these issues, there is hope for a more inclusive approach that honors the contributions of early residents and acknowledges their descendants' rightful claims to Canadian citizenship.

This flexible framework offers participants the chance to explore various employment opportunities across the country. item.title: Your Irish ancestors lived in Canada generations ago. You might still be Canadian
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada, Irish roots, ancestry, Cork, emigrants

Summary: In Canada, approximately 4.4 million people report having Irish roots, making Irish ancestry the third-largest in the country. This statistic reveals that nearly one in eight Canadians can trace their lineage back to Ireland, particularly to regions like Cork, Kerry, Wexford, and Tipperary. However, this number only accounts for those who remained in Canada. It does not reflect the many families who emigrated from Ireland over the years, leaving behind their roots for new opportunities. The cultural and historical connections between Canada and Ireland contribute to a rich tapestry of shared traditions and values. Recognizing both the presence of Irish descendants in Canada and the significance of those who migrated enhances understanding of the profound impact of Irish emigration on Canadian society. The legacy of Irish heritage continues to influence various aspects of life in Canada, from festivals and literature to culinary traditions, showcasing the enduring bond between the two nations. Thus, the story of Irish ancestry in Canada is not just about the living descendants but also about the memories and histories of those who crossed the ocean in search of a better life.

There are 4.4 million people in Canada who report Irish roots. item.title: Long-form birth certificates for Canadian citizenship by descent: what you need to know
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canadian citizenship, ancestry, documentation, birth certificates, application

Summary: If you suspect you might be eligible for Canadian citizenship through your ancestry, it's essential to begin gathering the necessary documentation for your application to Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC). One of the primary documents you will need is the birth certificate. However, you may encounter situations where you have two different types of birth certificates - the short form and the long form. Understanding the difference between these forms is crucial, as the long-form birth certificate typically contains more detailed information, including parental details and place of birth. This comprehensive information can significantly strengthen your citizenship application. Additionally, ensure you have any other required documents related to your lineage, such as marriage certificates or proof of your ancestor's immigration status. By methodically collecting these documents and verifying their authenticity, you can facilitate a smoother application process. Once you have all your information organized, you can move forward confidently with your application to IRCC, increasing your chances of a successful outcome in your pursuit of Canadian citizenship through ancestry.

You’ve just realized that you might be eligible for Canadian citizenship based on your ancestry. item.title: Two Canadian universities place among top 10 in global sustainability rankings
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Sustainability, Rankings, Universities, SDGs, Queen’s University

Summary: Times Higher Education (THE) has announced its 2026 Sustainability Impact Ratings, showcasing the commitment of universities toward sustainability. Notably, Queen’s University and the University of Alberta have achieved remarkable positions, landing in the global top 10. This ranking is a reflection of the universities' alignment with the United Nations' 17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), which encompass goals such as no poverty, climate action, and gender equality. The rankings serve as a tool to assess how well institutions integrate sustainability practices into their operations, research, and community engagement. It highlights the increasing importance of sustainability in higher education and encourages universities worldwide to pursue impactful practices that contribute to social, economic, and environmental sustainability. The recognition of Canadian institutions like Queen’s University and the University of Alberta reinforces their leadership in fostering an educational environment that prioritizes sustainable development. These universities set a benchmark for others to follow, promoting a global movement towards achieving sustainability in academia and beyond.

Times Higher Education (THE) has released its 2026 Sustainability Impact Ratings, with Queen’s University and the University of Alberta both landing in the global top 10. Whether you plan to purchase soon or are in the early stages of exploration, gathering information and understanding the mortgage landscape is essential. Taking the time to research and ask questions can make the homebuying journey smoother and more efficient, ultimately leading to a more confident purchasing experience. By being informed and prepared, newcomers can navigate the complexities of the Canadian real estate market with greater ease and assurance.

item.title: Ontario has launched new PNP pathways - YouTube
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Express Entry, Canada Immigration, mistakes, IRCC, updates

Summary: The video by Sahil Sayal highlights the top 10 mistakes to avoid when applying for Canada’s Express Entry program during 2026-2027. Key points include the importance of thorough documentation, emphasizing that incomplete or inaccurate applications can lead to delays or rejections. Candidates should ensure they meet the eligibility criteria, particularly in areas such as language proficiency, which is critical for scoring well in the Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS). Utilizing accurate job descriptions that match the National Occupational Classification (NOC) is also stressed to avoid further complications. Sayal advises on maintaining up-to-date information on Express Entry draws and remaining vigilant about potential changes in immigration policies announced by IRCC. Networking and acquiring references can enhance one’s profile, while not understanding the significance of educational credential assessments (ECA) can undermine a candidate’s application. Financial readiness is underscored, highlighting that candidates need to demonstrate their ability to support themselves and their families upon arrival in Canada. The discussion concludes with a reminder to seek professional advice and remain informed about new developments in immigration procedures, as updates are frequent and impactful. Following these guidelines can facilitate a smoother application process and improve chances for successful immigration to Canada.

Keywords: Provincial programs, PR, Express Entry, nomination, immigration

Summary: Provincial programs play a crucial role in the immigration process, providing pathways to Permanent Residency (PR) through the Express Entry system. Each province in Canada has the authority to nominate individuals for PR based on specific criteria that reflect their local labor market needs. When a province nominates a person, that individual receives additional points in the Express Entry Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS), significantly enhancing their chances of receiving an Invitation to Apply (ITA) for PR. The nomination process typically involves the province assessing the applicant's skills, work experience, and ability to contribute to the local economy. Candidates may need to meet specific eligibility requirements set forth by the province, such as prior work experience in a particular field or skills in high-demand occupations. This targeted approach allows provinces to attract skilled workers who can fill gaps in their workforce, ultimately supporting regional economic growth. In summary, provincial nomination enhances the immigration process by aligning individual qualifications with provincial labor market demands, offering a streamlined route for skilled workers to obtain PR in Canada.

There are ongoing calls for reforming the Express Entry system, which currently has limitations that could be addressed to better align with provincial goals and labor market demands. Changes under these proposals could lead to a more responsive immigration framework that accommodates the unique needs of provinces and fosters economic development across Canada. Summary: Employment and Social Development Canada (ESDC) has released its latest update on processing times for Labour Market Impact Assessments (LMIAs). This report indicates significant changes in wait times across various streams under the Temporary Foreign Worker Program (TFWP). Specifically, the wait times for LMIAs have increased for nearly all streams within the TFWP, suggesting a growing backlog or increased demand in those areas. However, a contrasting trend was noted in the permanent resident (PR) stream, which experienced an improvement of nearly one month in processing times. This shift could indicate the government's efforts to streamline the PR application process, providing a faster pathway for individuals seeking to settle permanently in Canada. For those interested in applying for work permits, ESDC encourages individuals to schedule a free consultation to understand their options better and navigate the complexities of the LMIA process. Overall, the update highlights the dynamic nature of employment-related immigration pathways and the varying impacts on different streams within Canada's labor market policies.

The move to strengthen verification processes indicates a commitment to maintaining the integrity of the immigration system and ensuring that applicants meet the necessary language requirements. By implementing these measures, immigration authorities aim to create a more robust framework for evaluating language proficiency and to deter fraudulent activities that compromise the application process.

item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada, citizenship, Minister Lena Diab, descent files, halted decisions

Summary: On Tuesday, June 23, Canadian Immigration Minister Lena Diab announced that the government has paused all decisions related to citizenship by descent applications. This decision comes as part of a review of existing files, specifically targeting those who have already received citizenship certificates. Diab indicated that the review process is thorough, with officials going through the cases to determine their legitimacy and compliance with regulations. Individuals whose files are found to be in order are being informed that their citizenship status is secure. This move aims to address concerns regarding the integrity of the citizenship application process and ensure that only eligible candidates attain citizenship by descent. The Immigration Ministry is committed to upholding the legal framework governing citizenship in Canada, emphasizing the importance of a fair and transparent process. Those awaiting decisions on their applications might experience delays as the review unfolds, but the Ministry is focused on resolving these matters in a systematic and efficient manner to maintain public trust in the immigration system.

Keywords: Maya, Canadian citizenship, envelope, rejection, daughter

Summary: Maya, a resident of Sacramento, received an unexpected envelope from Nova Scotia this spring. Initially hesitant to open it due to her belief that it contained a rejection letter, she ultimately discovered it held a Canadian citizenship certificate for her four-year-old daughter. This surprising news opened up new possibilities for their family, highlighting a connection to Canadian heritage. The emergence of her daughter's citizenship could potentially affect their family's future decisions, including travel, education, and identity. Maya was prompted to reconsider the significance of her family's lineage and explore options for anyone interested in determining if their ancestry qualifies for citizenship as well. The article concludes with a call to action for readers to check their family lineage and to offer a free consultation service to help them navigate the process of acquiring citizenship based on their heritage. This story illustrates the emotional journey of navigating familial ties and legal documentation, emphasizing the importance of connection to one's roots and the benefits it may bring to future generations.

When the envelope from Nova Scotia landed in her Sacramento mailbox this spring, Maya* almost didn’t open it. She was convinced it would be a rejection. Inside was a proof of Canadian citizenship certificate. It had her four-year-old daughter’s name on it. Find out if your family lineage qualifies and get a free consultation with […]

item.content:encoded:
Keywords: CoQ, Express Entry, Canada Immigration, Points, AHS Immigration

Summary: The Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS) in Canada’s Express Entry program assigns points based on various factors, including skills, work experience, and educational background. Obtaining a Certificate of Qualification (CoQ) can significantly enhance an applicant's profile by boosting their points in the Express Entry pool. This certificate demonstrates that the individual has met specific industry standards and possesses the necessary qualifications for their trade in Canada. Applicants aiming to increase their CRS score should consider obtaining a CoQ to improve their chances of receiving an Invitation to Apply (ITA) for permanent residency. It is important for potential immigrants to thoroughly research their eligibility for CoQ and prepare the required documentation. Additionally, consulting with immigration experts or firms, like AHS Immigration, can provide valuable insights and assistance throughout the application process. Overall, securing a CoQ not only strengthens a candidate's application but also provides greater opportunities for employment in skilled trades within Canada. Understanding the dynamics of the Express Entry system and capitalizing on helpful resources can lead to a successful immigration journey.

item.title: How Canada's skilled immigration system went off the rails – and how to fix it
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Express Entry, Canadian immigration, skilled labor, policy changes, immigration system

Summary: Express Entry was initially designed as a streamlined system to manage applications for Canadian skilled immigration, but its foundation was not solely focused on attracting skilled workers. Over the years, Express Entry has undergone significant transformations influenced by changes in labor market needs and political factors. The original intent was to create a fair and efficient process for immigrants, yet it has at times prioritized certain demographics and professions over others. This has led to an uneven impact on various sectors of the economy and has prompted critical discussions about the effectiveness of the immigration system. The program's structure has sparked debates regarding its accessibility and fairness, especially for skilled workers who may not fit into the narrow criteria established. As Canada continues to evolve its immigration policies, the future direction of Express Entry remains uncertain, with potential reforms aimed at addressing the emerging labor market demands and enhancing inclusivity. Ultimately, while Express Entry was not solely meant to serve as a Canadian skilled immigration program, it has become a pivotal element, reflecting the ongoing challenges and aspirations of Canada’s immigration landscape.
By Nino Melikidze and Steven Paolasini June 24, 2026. Express Entry was never meant to be a Canadian skilled immigration program.

item.title: Quebec announces July 2026 intake for family sponsorship, exempts adult dependent children from cap
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Quebec, family sponsorship, intake, applications, permanent residence

Summary: Quebec will re-open its intake for capped family sponsorship applications on July 2, 2026. Over a span of two years, the province will accept a maximum of 15,700 applications for individuals wishing to sponsor family members for permanent residence. The breakdown of these applications includes 13,300 specifically for spouses and 2,400 designated for parents or grandparents. Notably, the rules regarding dependent children have changed; dependent children aged 18 or older will no longer be subject to the same sponsorship restrictions as before. This adjustment aims to streamline the application process and address the needs of families wishing to reunite in Quebec. The provincial government is likely allowing this higher intake to accommodate the significant demand for family reunification within the province. Overall, this initiative reflects Quebec's ongoing commitment to fostering family ties and supporting its residents in bringing their loved ones to join them in Canada. Potential sponsors and applicants are encouraged to prepare for the intake period to ensure timely submission of their applications for consideration.

Quebec will re-open intake for capped family sponsorship applications on July 2, 2026. Over a two-year period, the province will accept a maximum of 15,700 applications for undertakings to sponsor family members for permanent residence: 13,300 for spouses, and 2,400 for parents or grandparents. Dependent children aged 18 or older will no longer be subject […]

item.title: Express Entry pool gets more top heavy
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Express Entry, profiles, candidates, growth rate, competitive

Summary: From May 24 to June 21, the trends in the Express Entry system revealed significant growth in top-ranking profiles. The overall pool experienced a modest increase of 0.33%, illustrating the gradual rise of candidates within the system. In sharp contrast, the number of candidates scoring 501 or higher surged impressively, escalating by 14.64%. This dramatic uptick indicates a competitive edge among individuals aiming for high-ranking profiles in the Express Entry system, showcasing a growth rate that is 4,400% higher than the overall pool. Such statistics reflect a shifting dynamic in the labor market and immigration landscape, as more candidates strive to enhance their scores and improve their chances for selection. The focus on scores within the 501–1,200 range suggests a nuanced competition, emphasizing the importance for prospective candidates to evaluate their own profiles and understand the criteria that distinguish top contenders. By assessing their competitiveness, individuals may strategize effectively to bolster their positions within the Express Entry framework, positioning themselves favorably in the selection process for permanent residency in Canada.

From May 24 to June 21, the number of top-scoring Express Entry profiles grew at a rate 4,400% faster than the overall pool. Overall, the draw is a key step for many aspiring immigrants looking to contribute to Canadian society.

Summary: Moving to Canada presents an exciting opportunity for skilled immigrants, but the journey to securing a first job can be daunting. Many newcomers arrive with exceptional qualifications and extensive work experience, yet they often face significant challenges in obtaining interview calls. One key factor that contributes to this difficulty is the importance of effective communication skills, particularly conversational English. It is crucial for immigrants to practice their language proficiency, not only for the CELPIP test but also for job interviews, as clear communication can significantly impact their chances of being hired. Additionally, understanding the job market landscape, networking, and tailoring resumes to meet Canadian standards are essential steps for improving job prospects. Overcoming barriers, such as unfamiliarity with local hiring practices and cultural differences, is vital for professionals seeking employment in their respective fields. By addressing these challenges head-on, skilled immigrants can enhance their chances of success and make a smoother transition into the Canadian workforce.

item.title: Federal–Provincial–Territorial Immigration Ministers meet to discuss sustainable immigration
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: FMRI, Immigration Levels Plan, Provincial Nominee Programs, labour shortages, PNP allocations

Summary: The Forum of Ministers Responsible for Immigration (FMRI) convened to discuss the 2027–2029 Immigration Levels Plan and to foster collaboration on federal–provincial–territorial (FPT) priorities. A significant focus of the meeting was on returning immigration to sustainable levels, taking into account various regional, rural, and northern contexts. The ministers acknowledged the pressing need to address labour shortages that affect key economic sectors across Canada. They emphasized the importance of supporting communities nationwide in their immigration strategies. Additionally, the role of Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs) was highlighted as crucial in meeting these goals. The ministers discussed the necessity for stable and adequate allocations of PNPs to enhance their effectiveness in addressing local labour market needs. Overall, the meeting aimed to align immigration goals with the economic and demographic realities of different regions, ensuring a balanced approach that benefits all Canadians. The discussions will help shape a collaborative framework for future immigration policies that respond to both national needs and local conditions.
The Forum of Ministers Responsible for Immigration (FMRI) met today to advance collaboration on the 2027–2029 Immigration Levels Plan and discuss federal–provincial–territorial (FPT) priorities. They discussed returning immigration to sustainable levels while considering regional, rural and northern contexts, addressing labour shortages, reinforcing key economic sectors, and supporting communities across the country. Ministers also discussed the role of Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs) in advancing these considerations and the need for stable and adequate PNP allocations.

The ministers emphasized the necessity of improving newcomer outcomes by facilitating better integration into the community and job market. This includes providing adequate support services and resources to help newcomers settle and thrive. Recommendations were made to streamline application processes and enhance communication between federal and provincial governments to achieve these goals. The PT ministers underscored the vital role that immigration plays in rejuvenating local economies and supporting demographic stability. They collectively called for greater flexibility within the Express Entry system that would allow provinces to attract immigrants who meet their unique labour market needs. Overall, the discussions aimed at not only improving the immigration system but also ensuring that it effectively benefits both newcomers and the communities they enter.

Summary: The Express Entry system is a pathway for skilled immigrants to become permanent residents in Canada. It operates through a points-based Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS) that assesses candidates based on factors like age, education, work experience, and language proficiency. The system periodically conducts draws, inviting the highest-ranking candidates to apply for permanent residency. In 2026, additional draws may take place, providing more opportunities for skilled workers. This approach aims to meet labor market needs and support economic growth. Candidates are encouraged to improve their CRS scores by obtaining higher language test results, additional education, or obtaining work experience, thereby enhancing their chances of being selected in future draws. The Express Entry program continues to evolve, adapting to immigration goals, labor shortages, and demographic changes in Canada. Understanding the nuances of the CRS and staying updated on draw results and criteria are essential for prospective applicants to navigate the immigration process successfully. As the program develops, it remains a vital tool for both Canada and skilled workers seeking to contribute to the country’s economy and society.

item.content:encoded:

Summary: The Canadian Experience Class (CEC) draw is proceeding as planned, with the latest round resulting in the issuance of 4,000 Invitations to Apply (ITAs) for permanent residency. This draw reflects the ongoing commitment of the Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) to facilitate immigration and address labor shortages in the country. The Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS) score in the latest draw decreased to 516, down from 518 in the previous round. This minor drop in score indicates a slight easing of competition among candidates, potentially allowing more individuals to qualify for immigration. Such draws are crucial for maintaining a steady influx of skilled workers, which is vital for Canada's economic growth and recovery, especially post-pandemic. The IRCC's approach underscores the importance of attracting talent with Canadian work experience, thereby benefiting both the immigrants and the Canadian economy. As immigration policies continue to evolve, prospective applicants are encouraged to stay informed about upcoming draws and changes in the CRS scoring system, maximizing their chances to secure an ITA in future rounds.

One key aspect of these updated guidelines is the need for a police certificate from the applicant's current country of residence. This clarification aims to streamline the application process and ensure that applicants present the necessary documentation. The updated requirements are intended to enhance security measures and maintain the integrity of the work permit application process. As applicants prepare for their IEC applications, they are advised to be aware of these changes and ensure they have the proper police verification documentation in place. This move is part of Canada's effort to facilitate international mobility while ensuring safety and compliance with immigration regulations.

item.title: How a Canadian great-grandparent can make you a citizen
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canadian, citizen, great-grandparent, apply, passport

Summary: If one of your great-grandparents was a Canadian citizen, you may qualify for Canadian citizenship regardless of where you, your parents, or your grandparents were born. This allows individuals who have no recent ties to Canada, such as holding a Canadian passport in generations, the opportunity to connect with their heritage. To gain citizenship, you need to establish your relationship with your Canadian great-grandparent through appropriate documentation. This process can provide you with the chance to become a Canadian citizen and access the rights and privileges associated with it. Once you gather the necessary proof of lineage, you can proceed to apply for citizenship. Being aware of this possibility can open doors for individuals seeking to explore their family history and gain Canadian nationality, thus potentially benefiting from Canada's resources, services, and community as citizens. This citizenship pathway emphasizes the significance of familial connections and may be an avenue for many who wish to embrace their Canadian roots.

If one of your great-grandparents was Canadian, you may now be a Canadian citizen—even if you, your parents, and your grandparents were all born abroad, and no one in the family has held a Canadian passport in generations. If you can prove your relation to your Canadian great-grandparent, you can apply for the document that […]

item.title: Canada gives second chance to asylum seekers from Uganda
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Uganda, Canada, protected person status, negative decision, measures

Summary: On June 19, new measures were announced that will benefit Ugandan nationals and former residents of Uganda facing imminent removal from Canada. The initiative aims to provide these individuals with a second chance to obtain protected person status. Specifically, it applies to those who received a final negative decision regarding their application between June 20, 2025, and June 19, 2026. This move is part of Canada's efforts to address the unique challenges and risks faced by Ugandans, particularly given the political and social climate in Uganda. Eligible individuals will have the opportunity to reapply and present their cases for consideration under these new regulations. This initiative reflects Canada's commitment to providing protection and support to individuals in need, ensuring that those who may have legitimate claims to refugee status are not unfairly removed from the country. The measures demonstrate the government’s responsive approach to changing circumstances and underlying human rights issues in Uganda, reaffirming Canada’s role in global humanitarian efforts.

Many individuals from Uganda who had faced imminent removal from Canada will be given a second chance at obtaining protected person status. item.title: How to request urgent processing of a Canadian citizenship certificate
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: proof of citizenship, IRCC, processing time, Bill C-3, Canadian

Summary: A proof of citizenship certificate is an important document for confirming Canadian citizenship. The processing time for this certificate through Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) currently stands at approximately 15 months. This lengthy wait time has increased recently due to a surge in applications. A significant number of applicants are American citizens who have become newly eligible for Canadian citizenship under Bill C-3, which took effect in December 2025. As a result, the demand for proof of citizenship has escalated, leading to a growing queue of applicants. The influx of applications reflects the changing landscape of citizenship eligibility in Canada, particularly driven by recent legislative changes. Individuals seeking to verify their Canadian citizenship are now facing a much longer waiting period, which highlights the pressing need for IRCC to address processing delays and improve service efficiency. Overall, the situation emphasizes the importance of being informed about the citizenship application process and the potential timelines involved.

A proof of citizenship certificate is the document that confirms you’re already Canadian. IRCC currently takes about 15 months to process one, and that wait has climbed as hundreds of thousands of people, many of them Americans newly eligible under Bill C-3, have joined the queue since the law took effect in December 2025. item.content:encoded:
